variables.r: Distance from pivot to point of force application (m)
variables.θ: Angle between position vector r and force F (deg)
variables.I: Moment of inertia about axis of rotation (kg·m²)

locked_facts: Only the perpendicular component of force creates torque
Torque depends on both force magnitude AND perpendicular distance
Angular momentum L = Iω, conserved when τ_net = 0
Moment of inertia I depends on mass distribution about axis
truth_statements: Torque is the turning effect of a force and equals the cross product of position vector and force
Only the component of force perpendicular to the position vector contributes to torque
The net torque equals the product of moment of inertia and angular acceleration
Angular momentum is conserved when the net external torque is zero
The moment of inertia depends on the mass and its distribution relative to the axis of rotation

aha_moment.what_to_show: Same force F applied at two different distances from the pivot — one close, one far. The torque bar for the far application point is much larger. A door analogy: pushing near the hinge vs near the handle.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two torque bars side by side — same F but different r — the longer r produces visibly larger torque
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that force alone does not determine torque — the distance from the pivot matters equally

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: A rigid rod free to rotate about a pivot. Force F applied at distance r from pivot. The perpendicular component F·sin(θ) and the parallel component F·cos(θ) are shown.
simulation_states.emphasis: Only the perpendicular component F⊥ = F·sin(θ) contributes to torque

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Force applied, rod begins to rotate. Torque value τ = rF·sin(θ) displayed. Angular acceleration α = τ/I shown. The rod speeds up.
simulation_states.emphasis: Torque causes angular acceleration — the rotational equivalent of F = ma

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Same force F applied at r₁ (near pivot) and r₂ (far from pivot). Two torque bars compared side by side. τ₂ >> τ₁. Door analogy: pushing near hinge vs near handle.
simulation_states.emphasis: Same force, different distance — the torque is DIFFERENT. Distance from pivot matters as much as force.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust force magnitude and distance from pivot. See torque update in real time. Try F at the pivot (r = 0): torque = 0 regardless of force.
simulation_states.emphasis: Move the force application point: at the pivot, torque is zero no matter how large the force. At maximum distance, torque is greatest.

common_misconceptions.belief: Torque depends only on force magnitude — a larger force always produces more torque
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT says torque τ = rF·sin(θ) depends on force, distance from pivot, AND the angle. A large force applied at the pivot produces zero torque.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Same force at two different distances from pivot — torque bars show dramatically different values

legend.meaning: Distance from pivot to force application

common_traps: applying force at pivot and expecting rotation
